coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of thrombomodulin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Liyan
Zhu, Jiaming
Wang, Wen
Jin, Lehe
Fu, Yanjiao
Duan, Bohui
Tan, Liang
coredata.dc:description: As an integral glycoprotein on the surface of endothelial cells, thrombomodulin (TM) has very high affinity for thrombin. TM has been regarded to be a marker of endothelial damage since it can be released during endothelial cell injury. In this work, a highly sensitive fluorescence method for the quantitative detection of TM was developed. TM antibody (Ab) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were bound on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to construct BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope and UV–vis spectrophotometry. The fluorescence of acridine orange (AO) was quenched by the prepared gold nanocomposites based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of TM, the fluorescence was turned on due to the effective separation of AO from the surface of gold nanocomposites. Under optimum conditions, the enhanced fluorescence intensity displayed a linear relationship with the logarithm of the TM concentration from 0.1pgmL−1 to 5ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 12fgmL−1. The release of soluble thrombomodulin (sTM) by the injured HUVEC-C cells in the presence of H2O2 was investigated using the proposed method. The released sTM content in the growth medium was found to be increased with the enhancement of contact time of the cells with H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thrombomodulin
Immunoreaction
Acridine orange
Gold nanocomposites
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis on sTM Release by Injured Vascular Endothelial Cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites. (B) Photos of AuNPs (a) and BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites suspension (b). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs (a, black line), BSA (b, blue line), TM Ab (c, pink line) and BSA-AuNPs-Ab (d, red line).

Fluorescence excitation (A) and emission (B) spectra of 33μM AO in pH6.5 PBS in the absence (a, a′) and presence of AuNPs (b, b′), BSA-AuNPs-Ab (c, c′) and the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and 5ngmL−1 TM (d, d′). Excitation slit: 5nm, emission slit: 5nm.

Schematic illustration for the detection of TM based on FRET. (A) Strong fluorescence of AO. (B) Fluorescence quenching due to FRET between AO and AuNPs. (B′) Fluorescence emission spectrum of AO (blue curve) and absorption spectrum of AuNPs (red curve). (C) Enhanced fluorescence due to the increased distance between AO molecules and BSA-AuNPs-Ab. (D) Recovery of fluorescence owing to the binding of TM with BSA-AuNPs-Ab.

The effects of pH (A) and the binding time of TM with BSA-AuNPs-Ab (B) on the difference between the fluorescence intensity of 33μM AO in the presence of BSA-AuNPs-Ab and that in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and 1ngmL−1 TM (ΔF). Results are presented as mean±SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

Fluorescence emission spectra of 33μM AO in pH6.5 PBS in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and TM with different concentrations. The concentration of TM (from a to h): 0, 1×10−4, 1×10−3, 1×10−2, 0.1, 0.5, 1 and 5ngmL−1. Insert: Plots of the fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) vs. the logarithm of the TM concentration (lgc TM). Results are presented as mean±SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

The difference between the fluorescence intensity of 33μM AO in pH6.5 PBS in the presence of BSA-AuNPs-Ab and that in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and 60ngmL−1 BSA (a), 60ngmL−1 glucose (b), 60ngmL−1 serine (c), 60ngmL−1 glycine (d), 60ngmL−1 riboflavin (e), 60ngmL−1 folic acid (f) and 3ngmL−1 TM (g).

(A–E) Microscope images (400×) of HUVEC-C cells in growth phase before (A) and after (B–E) the addition of 1mM H2O2. The contact time of HUVEC-C with H2O2: 2h (B), 8h (C), 16h (D) and 24h (E). (F) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity difference induced by sTM in growth medium (ΔF sTM) and the contact time of HUVEC-C with H2O2. Results are presented as mean±SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of thrombomodulin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an integral glycoprotein on the surface of endothelial cells, thrombomodulin (TM) has very high affinity for thrombin. TM has been regarded to be a marker of endothelial damage since it can be released during endothelial cell injury. In this work, a highly sensitive fluorescence method for the quantitative detection of TM was developed. TM antibody (Ab) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were bound on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to construct BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope and UV–vis spectrophotometry. The fluorescence of acridine orange (AO) was quenched by the prepared gold nanocomposites based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of TM, the fluorescence was turned on due to the effective separation of AO from the surface of gold nanocomposites. Under optimum conditions, the enhanced fluorescence intensity displayed a linear relationship with the logarithm of the TM concentration from 0.1pgmL−1 to 5ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 12fgmL−1. The release of soluble thrombomodulin (sTM) by the injured HUVEC-C cells in the presence of H2O2 was investigated using the proposed method. The released sTM content in the growth medium was found to be increased with the enhancement of contact time of the cells with H2O2.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is considered to be an important research tool since many advantages, such as high sensitivity, easy operation and rapid process, can be found in this method. A non-radiative transfer process of resonant fluorescence energy from an excited donor fluorophore to a proximal ground-state acceptor owing to the interaction of electric dipoles is normally called as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). It is reported that the optimal distance of a donor-acceptor pair for FRET is in the range of 2–9nm [1]. Hence, this technique is very sensitive to nanometer-scale changes in donor-acceptor distance and it has been successfully applied in analytical and bioanalytical chemistry [2–7]. Immunosensors employing FRET principle have been developed by assembling fluorescent donors/acceptors with biomolecules, such as antibodies and antigens. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been widely employed in numerous fields, including catalysis, diagnostics and biosensors [8,9] due to their excellent optical and electronic properties. This nanomaterial is regarded as a promising acceptor in FRET-based immunoassays [10–14] owing to its high extinction coefficient and a broad absorption spectrum in visible region, which can be overlapped by the emission spectra of usual energy donors [15].

Endothelial cells (ECs) in direct contact with blood are named as vascular endothelial cells (VECs) and they play a crucial role in the control of vascular function. Vascular endothelial cell injury (VEC injury) is one of key factors for some inflammatory events that ultimately result in vascular damage. Thrombomodulin (TM) is a transmembranous glycoprotein acting as a receptor for thrombin [16] and serves as an anticoagulant protein on the blood vessel wall [17]. In order to maintain homeostasis and to ensure a rapid and localized hemostatic and inflammatory response to injury, expression of TM on the surface of VECs is tightly regulated [18]. Soluble thrombomodulin (sTM), which has been isolated from human blood and urine [19], is a cleaved form of tissue TM with loss of parts of the transmembrane domain and the cytoplasmatic tail [20]. Some clinical studies show that various diseases associated with VEC injury or proteolytic activity on the surface of VECs can lead to the increase of plasma levels of sTM [21,22]. TM and sTM have been regarded as markers of endothelial damage in vitro [17,23,24]. The evaluation of TM (or sTM) can provide important information for early diagnosis and therapy of vasculopathy including phlebophlogosis, atherosclerosis and thrombus.

To our knowledge, to date there are no reports on the determination of TM and the investigation on the release of sTM by VECs using FRET technique. In this paper, TM antibody (Ab) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were bound to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to construct BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope and UV–vis spectrophotometry. The fluorescence quenching of AO by gold nanocomposites was investigated. Highly sensitive detection of TM was performed based on the fluorescence recovery of the AO-nanocomposites system in the presence of TM. The injury of VECs induced by H2O2 was achieved and the release of sTM by injured VECs was analyzed using the proposed fluorescence method.

Thrombomodulin and rabbit anti-human TM antibody (TM Ab) were purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as received. Acridine orange (AO, B.S., dye content >99%) was purchased from Xiya Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Linshu, China) and its stock solution (0.5mM) was stored in the dark due to the sensitivity of the dye. HAuCl4 and trisodium citrate were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). The buffer for the assay was 0.1M phosphate buffer saline (PBS), prepared by mixing stock standard solution of Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4. Other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared in Milli-Q ultrapure water.

All fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The sizes of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites were characterized ex situ by a Tecnai G2 20ST transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA). The cell-modality observation was performed with an Eclipse TiS inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan).

The citrate-reduction method was employed to prepare colloidal AuNPs. A 50mL aqueous solution of 0.01% HAuCl4 (w/w) was firstly heated to boiling in a round-bottom flask. 2mL of 1% trisodium citrate was then added drop by drop under vigorous stirring condition. The color of the solution changed from grey, blue, purple, to wine red, which means the formation of gold nanoparticles. Finally, the mixed solution was stirred continuously until it was cooled to room temperature. The prepared gold nanoparticles were stored in a brown glass bottle at 4°C for further use.

The synthesis of BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposite suspension was performed as following: 1mL of Ab (75μgmL−1) was slowly added into 10mL AuNPs suspension (adjusted to pH7.4 with 0.1M NaOH) under stirring condition and then the mixture was continuously stirred for 4h at 4°C. Next, 2.75mL of 5% BSA solution was added to cover the nonspecific sites on the surface of AuNPs. After stirring for 4h, the solution was centrifuged at 13,000rpm for 30min. Finally, the purplish red precipitate was re-dispersed in 10mL of 0.1% BSA solution. Some antibody-gold nanoparticle composites prepared by this method have been reported [25,26]. Antibodies and BSA could be bound to gold nanoparticles based on AuS bonding rather than physical adsorption of these biomolecules.

All experiments, except where specified, were carried out at pH6.5 PBS. 50μL of BSA-AuNPs-Ab was mixed with 10μL of TM with different concentration and the mixture was incubated for 150min at ambient temperature. 180μL of buffer solution, 20μL of AO stock solution and the above solution comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and TM were added successively into a 0.5-mL polyethylene centrifugal tube. The final volume of mixture was adjusted to 300μL with ultrapure water. The mixture was subjected to fluorescence wavelength scans using a 300-μL micro quartz cell with the following instrumental parameters: excitation wavelength (λ ex), 497nm; excitation slit (EX), 5nm; emission wavelength range (λ em), 510–650nm; emission slit (EM), 5nm.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cell line, HUVEC-C cells were obtained from Xiangya School of Medicine, Central South University, China and they were incubated using DMEM/low glucose growth medium (HyClone) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), in an incubator (5% CO2, 37°C). Cells were seeded in some wells on a 96-well plate at the concentration 5×104 cellsmL−1. After 12h when the cells spread over the substrate, 10μL of H2O2 was added into the wells and the cell culture was continued. Several hours later, 10μL of growth medium was transferred into 50μL of BSA-AuNPs-Ab suspension using a micropipettor. The mixture was added into pH6.5 PBS containing AO. The fluorescence measurement was performed using the same instrumental parameters in the detection of TM.

Fig. 1A shows the TEM image of the prepared BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites. These nanocomposites were highly monodispersed with the average diameter of 15nm. Fig. 1B shows the photos of gold nanoparticles and BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites suspension. One can find the wine red colloidal gold solution. By contrast, the nanocomposite suspension was in purplish red color, which indicates the different optical characteristic of these nanocomposites due to the assembly of biomacromolecules on the surface of AuNPs. Fig. 1C shows the UV–vis spectra of AuNPs, BSA, TM Ab and BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites in aqueous solution from 200 to 800nm, respectively. AuNPs had a characteristic absorbance peak at about 520nm. BSA and TM Ab presented almost the same absorption curves. A prominent absorption peak at 277nm, which is attributed to Tyr and Trp residues [27], can be found on the spectra of them. The spectrum of BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites appeared to be an overlapping of two spectra for AuNPs and proteins accompanied with obvious red shift of the absorption peak in the visible region.

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of AO in pH6.5 PBS in the absence and presence of AuNPs, BSA-AuNPs-Ab and the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and TM. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission intensity of AO can be observed at 497nm and 539nm, respectively (curves a and a′). They were significantly decreased in the presence of AuNPs (curves b and b′). It is reported that the potenz value of the conventional acid dissociation constant, pK a, of acridine orange was 9.6 [28]. Hence, AO molecules were positively charged at pH6.5. As shown in Fig. 3B, positively-charged AO could be adsorbed on the surface of citrate-stabilized AuNPs through electrostatic interactions. It is found from Fig. 3B′ that the emission spectrum of AO was partly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of AuNPs. The value of critical transfer distance (Förster distance, R DA) between AO and AuNPs was calculated using Eq. (1) [29],
(1)
R DA 6
=
9000 ×
ln 10
k p 2
Ω DA
ϕ D
128
N A
π 5
n 4
where k p 2 is the orientation factor, equal to 2/3 for randomly distributed fluorophores, Ω DA is overlap integral for overlapping fluorescence spectra of the donor (AO) with absorption spectra of the acceptor (AuNPs), Φ D is quantum yield of the donor (0.2 for AO [30]), N A is Avogadro constant, n is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for H2O). Ω DA could be obtained by using Eq. (2):
(2)
Ω DA
=
∫ 0 ∞
f d
λ
ε a
λ
λ 4
dλ
∫ 0 ∞
f d
λ
dλ
where
∫ 0 ∞
f d
λ
ε a
λ
λ 4
dλ is the area of overlapping region,
∫ 0 ∞
f d
λ
dλ is area of the fluorescence and λ is the wavelength of the overlapping region. The Förster distance between AO and AuNPs was calculated to be 4.02nm, which suggests an efficient FRET between the donor and the acceptor. As a result, AO on the surface of AuNPs displayed weak emission due to the highly efficient fluorescence quenching ability of AuNPs. One can observe from Fig. 2 that curves c and c′ presented the increased fluorescence intensity in comparison with curve b and b′. It indicates that the fluorescence quenching ability of gold nanocomposites was inferior to that of AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 3C, the immobilization of TM Ab and BSA on AuNPs resulted in the steric hindrance effect, leading to the increased distance between the dye molecules and gold nanocomposites. As a result, FRET was actually weakened. It is interesting to be found that the fluorescence of the AO-gold nanocomposites system was significantly recovered/turned on in the presence of TM (curves d and d′ in Fig. 2). The experimental results suggest that the binding of TM on BSA-AuNPs-Ab based on the immunoreaction put a serious obstacle in the above-mentioned FRET. As shown in Fig. 3D, the relatively strong fluorescence of AO re-appeared with the effective separation of the dye molecules from the gold nanocomposites.

The recovery of the AO fluorescence in the presence of TM was employed to detect TM in this work. Condition optimization for the TM measurement was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of AO in the presence of BSA-AuNPs-Ab (F 1) and that in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and TM (F 2) were measured, respectively, and their difference, ΔF (ΔF = F 2 − F 1) was obtained. Fig. 4 shows the effects of pH and the binding time of TM with BSA-AuNPs-Ab on the ΔF value. It is found from panel A that the fluorescence intensity difference was increased with the enhancement of the pH from 5.5 to 6.5, and then began to decrease rapidly at the higher pH values. It is reported that the isoelectric point of TM is around pH4 [31]. The electrostatic repulsion might exist between negatively charged TM and citrate-stabilized AuNPs in the solution with the relatively high pH value, which went against the binding between TM and the gold nanocomposites. As a result, the effective separation of AO molecules from the gold nanocomposites could be not completely realized. As the maximum ΔF value was achieved at pH6.5, this pH was employed in all the subsequent experiments. The binding time of TM with BSA-AuNPs-Ab was also investigated. One can observe from panel B that the ΔF value was gradually enhanced with the increase of the binding time from 30 to 150min and then remained almost unchanged when the latter was further increased. It indicates that the adequate amount of TM would be bound on BSA-AuNPs-Ab and more AO molecules could be separated from the gold nanocomposites if the binding time was long enough. Thus, the optimal binding time of TM was selected at 150min.

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of AO in pH6.5 PBS in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and TM with different concentrations. The fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced when the TM content in the mixture was increased, suggesting the recovery of the AO fluorescence owing to the binding of TM with BSA-AuNPs-Ab and the separation of AO from the gold nanocomposites. As shown in the insert, the ΔF value exhibited a linear response with respect to lgc TM over the TM concentration from 0.1pgmL−1 to 5ngmL−1. The regression equation was ΔF =60.1lgc TM +316 with a high correlation coefficient of 0.986 and the detection limit was found to be 12fgmL−1 estimated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Hence, a highly sensitive turn-on fluorescence method for the quantitative detection of TM could be developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis on sTM Release by Injured Vascular Endothelial Cells
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the present method was evaluated by analysis on soluble thrombomodulin in growth medium since this cleaved form of tissue TM could be released by injured vascular endothelial cells. To investigate the specificity of the proposed method, the effects of some co-existing components in DMEM growth medium supplemented with 10% FBS, for example, BSA, glucose, serine, glycine, riboflavin and folic acid, on the fluorescence detection were evaluated, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . The difference between the fluorescence intensity of AO in pH6.5 PBS in the presence of BSA-AuNPs-Ab and that in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and 3ngmL−1 TM or the above interfering substances was measured, respectively. The fluorescence intensity change induced by TM was found to be further larger than that caused by these interfering substances with 20-fold concentration, which suggests that there was a specific reaction between TM and the gold nanocomposites and the proposed FRET immunosensor exhibited a high selectivity for the detection of TM.

It is known that H2O2 may decompose into hydroxyl radicals that readily damage vital cellular components via oxidative stress. Accumulation of H2O2 in cells can lead to cell injury through base modifications and strand breakage in genomic DNA as well as apoptosis induction. H2O2 was selected to stimulate the release of sTM by VECs in the present work. It was added into the growth medium when HUVEC-C cells were in growth phase and its final concentration was 1mM. Fig. 7A–E show the microscope images of HUVEC-C cells incubated in growth medium at the different time before and after the addition of H2O2. HUVEC-C cells without H2O2-stimulation were spindle-shaped (panel A). They were linked to one another and basically distributed on the substrate as a monolayer. The obvious shape-change of the cells could be observed at 2h after the addition of H2O2 (panel B). The cell shrinking resulted in the irregularly polygon-shaped vascular endothelial cells. This changing trend was more and more apparent with the increase of contact time of the cells with H2O2. At 8h, most of HUVEC-C cells were separated from each other, presenting the decreased spreading area (panel C). Sixteen hours later, a large number of flat circle-like vesicles were presented on the substrate and some cell debris released by dead cells could be found (panel D). Lots of cell debris rather than living cells were located on the substrate after 24h (panel E). The above results suggest that HUVEC-C cells suffered serious injury in the presence of H2O2. Growth medium in the HUCEC-C incubation well after the addition of H2O2 was employed to investigate the release of sTM by the injured cells. The fluorescence intensity of AO in pH6.5 PBS in the presence of the mixture comprising BSA-AuNPs-Ab and growth medium in cell incubation well shown in Fig. 7A–E was measured and they were recorded as F A, F B, F C, F D and F E, respectively. These fluorescence intensity values were found to be increased in sequence. The difference between the first (F A) and the latter four (F B − F E) of the five F values, which was defined as ΔF sTM, was calculated, respectively, and the relationship between the obtained four ΔF sTM values and the contact time of HUVEC-C cells with H2O2 was shown in Fig. 7F. The ΔF sTM value was proportional to the contact time in the range from 2 to 24h with a correlation coefficient of 0.999. The above experimental results confirm the gradual release of sTM from the injured HUVEC-C cells under the durative stimulation of H2O2. These sTM molecules in growth medium could be bound to BSA-AuNPs-Ab, leading to the decreased efficiency of FRET between AO and the gold nanocomposites. The longer the contact time of the cells with H2O2 was, the more sTM molecules were released. As a result, more AO molecules were detached from the gold nanocomposites and they presented the increased fluorescence intensity.

BSA-AuNPs-Ab nanocomposites were successfully prepared and they could lead to the fluorescence quenching of AO due to FRET to a certain extent. The fluorescence recovery of the AO-gold nanocomposites system was achieved in the presence of TM owing to the increased distance between AO molecules and the gold nanocomposites. The florescence intensity difference of AO was closely related to the TM concentration, which developed a highly sensitive turn-on fluorescence sensor for the determination of TM. The proposed fluorescence method was applied in the analysis on the release of sTM by the H2O2-injured HUVEC-C cells. The sTM content in growth medium was found to be enhanced with the increase of the contact time of the cells with H2O2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14A095) and the Open Sustentation Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2014007).
